بَيْتُ الْحِكْمَة — House of Wisdom

K–12 Islamic Education Platform

Five layers · Two tracks · 13 grades · 390 weeks · Sacred learning integrated

tracks · one institution

In 8th-century Baghdad, Caliph Harun al-Rashid founded Bayt al-Hikmah — the House of Wisdom — the greatest center of learning the world had ever seen. Scholars of every faith translated, debated, and advanced knowledge in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy. Al-Khwarizmi invented algebra within its walls.

This platform carries that tradition forward — one family, one week, one Juz at a time.

Five layers

Curriculum · Studios · Pedagogy · Sacred · Digital tools

📜

Layer 1 — Curriculum

390 weeks · 5 subjects · audited against CCSS & NGSS

390 weeks of curriculum across 13 grades and 5 subjects — Mathematics, ELA, Writing, Science, and Social Studies — with Islamic scholars woven throughout as the actual history of each discipline. Every week connects to a Quranic Juz, a scholar, and an opening question that drives the week's inquiry. The curriculum meets CCSS and NGSS standards while restoring what those standards omit: the Islamic roots of algebra, astronomy, medicine, optics, and geography. Browse the full scope and sequence in the Curriculum tab.

🏛

Layer 2 — Studios

10 enrichment studios · backward-planned

Each studio has a peak task, a prerequisite map, and a direct connection to specific curriculum weeks. Tuesday open studio — all ages, all studios simultaneously — is the irreplaceable physical moment the platform is designed around.

MathAstronomyEngineeringMedicineHumanities
م

Layer 3 — Pedagogy

7 approaches from the House of Wisdom tradition

Sahifa, halaqa, translation chain, munazara, maktaba, murshid, isnad — each drawn from actual Islamic scholarly practice. The Murshid tab gives you live access to the AI murshid. Provide your API key and use it now.

✦

Layer 4 — Sacred layer

Name · Hadith · Muhasaba · Mathnawi · Istajibullah

Not a separate subject. The Name sets the lens (Saturday evening). The hadith holds the mirror (Wednesday). The muhasaba asks who you are becoming (Thursday). Browse all 30 Names mapped to all 30 Juz in the Sacred Layer tab.

🖥

Layer 5 — Digital tools

5 standalone tools · all downloadable · offline-capable

Sahifa (living manuscript), Murshid (AI scholarly guide), Halaqa (learning circle), Peer Teaching Library (knowledge commons), and Studio Digital Twin (maktaba of making). Each tool functions independently and connects to the others through the student's own record of learning.

Digital tools — all downloadable

Seven pedagogical approaches

From the House of Wisdom tradition · made operational

"They did not have teachers. They had questions that demanded answers, texts that demanded translation, problems that demanded solutions — and each other."

١

The Sahifa — living manuscript

Stronger online than physical

Every student maintains one continuous document across 13 years. Revision history never deleted. The Thursday revision entry proves learning happened. The muhasaba (private, never assessed) shows who was doing the learning. Parent accounts have optional access to their own sahifa — an invitation to co-inquiry, never required.

٢

The Halaqa — learning circle

Most impacted online — rebuilt with three substitutions

Physical: bodies in a circle. Online: pre-written commitment, named inquiry (facilitator reads every pre-answer and names specific thinking), 6-student limit. The AI halaqa implements all three. Text-only — no video removes performance anxiety and returns language to primacy.

٣

The Translation chain — peer teaching

Stronger online — reaches across years and geographies

The best moment to explain something is right after you understood it — when confusion is still fresh. Older students teach younger ones. The peer teaching library records these permanently. An online Grade 9 student's teaching is found by a physical Grade 7 student two years later.

٤

The Munazara — structured debate

Two forms online: asynchronous 48hr written + live video

A student defends a position against a rigorous opponent. The murshid prepares students for both forms. Knowing something means being able to defend it against the best counterargument. A student who loses a munazara having prepared well has demonstrated more than one who wins without preparation.

٥

The Studios — maktaba of making

Hardest online — solved by home kits + digital twin

The physical making that cannot be simulated. For online students: home kits sent by post, holiday camps for studio immersion, the digital twin for documentation. Tuesday's open studio — all ages, all studios simultaneously — is the irreplaceable physical school moment.

٦

The Murshid — AI scholarly guide

Native to online · four modes

Reads the student's full sahifa. Four modes: open inquiry, challenge, munazara prep, thread-finding. Never gives an answer before asking what was tried. Always ends with one question. The answer you arrive at yourself is permanent; the answer given to you is borrowed and will fade. Try it in the Murshid tab.

٧

The Isnad — chain of transmission

Stronger online — builds automatically from tool activity

Every peer teaching, build-on contribution, and sahifa thread is automatically recorded. By graduation, the isnad is an interactive map of intellectual transmission spanning 13 years. The graduation ceremony names every link aloud, regardless of track.

Weekly schedule

Sunday through Thursday · sacred layer integrated · select a day to explore

The sacred layer — 99 Names mapped to 30 Juz

Browse all 30 Names · each with story and carrying question

"Knowledge can be transmitted. Wisdom must be alchemised. The Name, the hadith, and the muhasaba are not curriculum content. They are the conditions in which the alchemy becomes possible."

✦Select a Name to explore its story and carrying question

Assessment framework

Formative and summative in parallel · without corrupting the muhasaba

"The Thursday revision proves that knowledge changed. The muhasaba asks whether the person changed. These are different questions. Only one can be assessed. Both need asking."

Summative — Tuesday weekly test · AI-generated · auto-marked · graded

Mastery of Sunday and Monday learning objectives

5 subjects · 22 minutes each · 2-hour continuous block — strict timer per subject; student reviews if finished early; no transitions.
AI-generated questions — calibrated to grade level; includes MCQ, short answer, editing tasks, writing prompts, and source analysis.
Auto-marking — MCQ and editing tasks auto-marked immediately; short answers and written responses marked by facilitator on Wednesday using AI-generated rubric.
Wednesday review — test results drive the murshid session; misconceptions corrected; Maths & Science first, then ELA, Writing & Social Studies.
Weekly grade report — AI-generated per-subject comments with sacred layer framing; printable; visible to facilitator and parent.

Formative — weekly, ongoing, never graded as a number

What is measured as learning happens

Sahifa revision entry (Thursday) — depth of learning, self-assessment of what changed.
Opening question pre-answer (Sunday) — baseline and misconception capture. Compared to Thursday's revision to show the week's distance.
Teach It — lesson annotation (Sunday & Monday) — every lesson ends with the student teaching it back. Drawing on each slide, writing explanations in their own words, asking the Ustadh where they are stuck. In the tradition of Bayt al-Hikmah, where scholars were evaluated not by examinations but by their ability to transmit: the student who can teach has understood; the student who cannot has only memorised. The annotated deck is published to the Peer Teaching Library as a permanent record of transmission.
Peer teaching library record — mastery evidence stronger than any test. The unanswered question is the most valuable data point. Ibn Khaldun observed that teaching is the most reliable test of knowledge — this library is the school's living evidence of that principle in practice.
Munazara record (Wednesday) — reasoning quality, intellectual honesty, ability to defend under pressure.
Studio annotation — mathematical annotation connecting physical making to curriculum concepts.
Murshid conversation log — what questions the student asked, what they tried, where understanding stopped.

Summative — milestone moments

What is measured at completion points

Semester portfolio (twice yearly) — student-curated: 3 sahifa entries + one peer teaching + one studio annotation + one munazara. Reviewed with the student.
End-of-year benchmark (Week 30) — standards-aligned, formal, graded. The only week with a conventional assessment.
Isnad ceremony portfolio (graduation) — not graded, witnessed. The chain read aloud. Who taught you, who you taught, what you built on.
Capstone project (G12 W19–28) — original investigation, oral defence, panel. The student brings their full 13-year sahifa.

Never assessed — by institutional commitment

What belongs to the student alone

The muhasaba — private always. Assessed muhasaba is not muhasaba.
The student's relationship with the Name of the week.
Spiritual development of any kind.
Quality of silence in a halaqa.
Private sahifa entries.
Whether the hadith changed anything. Whether the Mathnawi moved them.
The framework preserves a fundamental asymmetry: much can be seen, some cannot. Facilitators see revision entries, studio annotations, peer teaching records, and munazara reasoning. They do not see the muhasaba or the student's relationship with the sacred layer. This asymmetry is not a gap — it is the system's most important design decision.

House of Wisdom
Sign in to your household account

No account? Contact your administrator.

🏫

Institutional schools

New founding schools and established schools. Curriculum licensing, facilitator formation, studio para-facilitators supplied.

🏡

Homeschool & microschools

Families and networks of 5–15 students. Online platform, parent-facilitator formation, regional studio hub access.

🌙

Afterschool & camps

Revenue-generating from day one. Hosted at local schools. Gives homeschool students belonging. Proof of concept for institutional partnerships.

What the platform provides

Complete K–12 curriculum with Islamic heritage woven in
Facilitator formation programme (5 stages) for existing teachers
Studio para-facilitators employed by the platform — not the school
All five digital tools for students and facilitators
Afterschool & camps programme operated at the school's location

What the platform does not ask

Does not ask the school to hire more staff
Does not require a new building or new classrooms
Does not replace existing teachers — it transforms how they teach
Does not impose a conventional assessment regime on the sacred layer

Priority: new founding schools (just launching) adopt more easily than established schools with prior culture.

The homeschool model

The platform resolves the two deepest homeschooling problems simultaneously: the confidence gap (the murshid carries the instructional load; the Saturday Istajibullah session restores the parent's natural authority as co-inquirer) and the isolation problem (the AI halaqa, the peer teaching library, and the regional studio hub connect homeschool students across geographies).

AI HalaqaSaturday IstajibullahMurshidRegional hub

Microschool networks

5–15 students, mixed ages, parent-facilitators. The platform's cross-age halaqa is native to the microschool context. The regional studio hub serves multiple microschool networks simultaneously. A network of 10 microschools in a region has 80–120 students — enough to justify a permanent hub. The knowledge commons connects them all regardless of location.

How afterschool programs use the platform

The HOW platform adapts naturally to afterschool and camp settings. Rather than delivering a full school day, afterschool programs can draw on specific components — studios, halaqa, peer teaching, or Munazara — as standalone enrichment sessions that connect to whatever the student is studying during the day.

Open studio sessions — students from any school work on the same discipline simultaneously
Halaqa circles — cross-age, text-based inquiry that requires no prior curriculum alignment
Holiday camps: 5-day studio immersions in Astronomy, Civil Engineering, or Pharmacopoeia
Peer Teaching Library access — students browse and contribute regardless of their school setting

What makes it work in afterschool

The platform's modular design means an afterschool program can run one component well without needing the full curriculum. A studio session once a week builds a portfolio over a school year. A weekly halaqa builds the habit of named inquiry. The Munazara can be introduced as a debate club. Each component is complete in itself while connecting to the larger knowledge commons.

Every participant — regardless of entry point — contributes to the peer teaching library and studio digital twin. The knowledge commons grows from every direction.
